components.title: Learning Management System
components.description: The Learning Management System (LMS) hosts training content, courses, and assessments for staff and, where applicable, external learners. Access is role-based — Anonymous (unauthenticated visitors with read-only access to public content), User (registered learners), and Administrator (course and user management) — with authentication required for any role beyond Anonymous.
components.purpose: Deliver and track staff training content, courses, and assessments securely.

control-implementations.description: Control implementations for Learning Management System.

implemented-requirements.description: Users must authenticate with a valid account before accessing any role beyond Anonymous — course content, assessment submission, and administration functions are all gated behind successful authentication.
Multi-factor authentication is required for all registered User and Administrator accounts before access to the LMS is granted.

Administrator privileges (course creation, user management, system configuration) are limited to the personnel whose role requires them; standard learners are provisioned with the User role only, which grants no administrative capability.

LMS application, authentication, and administrative events (logins, failed logins, account creation, course/content changes, configuration changes) are forwarded to the organisation's centralised event logging facility for retention and analysis.

Every LMS account is assigned to a single, individually-attributable user — shared or generic accounts are not used, so every action recorded in the audit log can be attributed to one accountable individual.

control-implementations.description: Control implementations for Learning Management System.

implemented-requirements.description: The LMS provisions accounts under a small set of defined roles (Anonymous, User, Administrator), each granted only the access appropriate to that role. Account creation, modification, and removal is restricted to Administrator accounts and is itself logged.

Access to LMS objects (courses, assessments, user records, configuration) is enforced by the platform's built-in role-based access control — a user's role determines exactly which pages and functions they can reach, checked on every request.
Each LMS user account is assigned a unique identifier at creation, generated by the platform and never reused, so user actions remain traceable to a specific account for the lifetime of the system.

LMS authenticators (passwords) are required to meet the organisation's password complexity policy, are stored using a salted one-way hash, and are never transmitted or displayed in cleartext; users are required to set their own password on first login rather than continuing to use a system-generated default.

The LMS generates audit events for authentication attempts (success and failure), account lifecycle changes, course and content creation/modification, and administrative configuration changes, sufficient to reconstruct who did what and when.

A documented baseline configuration for the LMS platform, its installed modules/plugins, and its hosting environment is maintained and version-controlled; changes to the baseline follow the organisation's change management process before being applied.
